articles.title: Statistical Data Visualization
articles.contents:
Contents
Syllabus
Live Sessions
Homework
Readings
Lectures

This course explores the role of visualization within data science workflows. We’ll get hands-on experience how visualization can be used to support,
Data cleaning and tidying
Exploratory analysis
Model inspection
Scientific reporting
The course mostly uses R, though occasionally we will use the vega-lite library in javascript. Prior experience with R, but not javascript, is expected.
In terms of content, we’ll begin with a three week crash course on the fundamental principles of data visualization. This will provide a vocabulary for designing and critiquing visualizations throughout the semester. Weeks 4 - 5 are focused on the connections between visualization and tidy data. Rearranging your data in the right way can make it much easier to visualize; conversely, a good interface can immediately highlight data quality issues.
From here on out, we’ll focus on exploratory analysis and model visualization. Weeks 6 - 8 consider data with temporal, geographic, or network structure – each comes with its own visual conventions. Weeks 9 - 11 focus on visualization of high-dimensional data, drawing from ideas in unsupervised learning. Weeks 12 and 13 describe the recent use of visualization to support inspection of complex supervised models. We close with a discussion about the possibilities of visualization in the broader intellectual landscape.
Syllabus
This will be an asynchronous class, with short video recordings released in advance. There will be an optional discussion every week, working through supplementary readings and examples. Online discussion on the course piazza is encouraged.
Structurally, the main types of work in this class are
Homeworks. These will be released every other Friday, and focus on practical skill development.
Exams. There will be two midterms (open book, timed).
Group Project. This is an opportunity to explore a dataset or visualization algorithm.
The official syllabus, which gives requirements, learning objectives, and grading mechanisms, is available here.

(1) Intro to Data Science: Chapter 34 (2) Superheat Vignette (2, 3 & 6)  (3) Cluster Analysis of Genomic Data

(1) Beginner’s Guide to Dimensionality Reduction  (2) An Illustrated Introduction to the t-SNE algorithm  (3) How to use t-SNE effectively

(1) Text mining with R: Chapter 6  (2) Termite: Visualization Techniques for Assessing Textual Topic Models  (3) Visualizing the structure of RNA-seq expression data using grade of membership models
12

(1) Towards better analysis of machine learning models: A visual analytics perspective  (2) [Visualization in Bayesian workflow
](https://arxiv.org/abs/1709.01449)


13 
Visualizing Deep Learning Models
(1) Four Experiments in Handwriting with a Neural Network  (2) Towards Better Analysis of Deep Convolutional Networks
14 
Conclusion
(1) 655 Frustrations of Doing Data Visualization  (2) Tukey, Design Thinking, and Better Questions  (3) As We May Think
